dating.basis: Stratigraphy plus a long radiocarbon series from the French Archaeological Mission excavations (Jarrige et al., 1974–2000).

scientific.result: Aceramic Neolithic Period I dated to c. 7000–5500 BCE across multiple charcoal and bone samples.

source.label: Jarrige, C. et al., Mehrgarh: Field Reports 1974–1985 (1995)
source.detail: Excavation reports of the Department of Culture and Tourism, Sindh, with the C14 series.

scientific.result: Drilled human molars from graves dated between 9,000 and 7,500 years BP — the earliest evidence of proto-dentistry anywhere.

source.label: Coppa, A. et al., "Early Neolithic tradition of dentistry", Nature 440 (2006)

summary: A Neolithic settlement on the Kacchi plain (Balochistan), at the foot of the Bolan Pass: the earliest known farming and herding village in South Asia, with wheat and barley cultivation, cattle domestication, mudbrick architecture, ornament craft — and eleven drilled molar crowns in nine adults, worked with flint drill heads: the oldest evidence of dentistry in the human record.
whyItMatters: Mehrgarh anchors the deep-time start of the atlas. It shows continuous settled life on the subcontinent ~9,000 years ago and is widely treated as a precursor tradition to the Indus Valley Civilization — the substrate on which everything later in this atlas stands.
honestyNote: Mehrgarh is pre-Vedic and pre-literate. Calling it 'Hindu' would itself be an overclaim (see ledger category 9); equally, calling it 'ancient Pakistan' as a civilizational identity is an anachronism (see ledger category 7). It belongs to the shared deep history of the subcontinent, and its physical custodianship is Pakistani.

locations.name: Mehrgarh, Kacchi District, Balochistan, Pakistan
locations.note: approximate; site is unexcavated backfill today

sources.label: Jarrige, J.-F. & Meadow, R., "The Antecedents of Civilization in the Indus Valley", Scientific American 243 (1980)
Coppa, A. et al., "Early Neolithic tradition of dentistry", Nature 440 (2006)
Possehl, G., The Indus Civilization: A Contemporary Perspective (AltaMira, 2002)
Kenoyer, J.M., Ancient Cities of the Indus Valley Civilization (OUP, 1998)
